summary: Graham argues that falling startup costs make traditional hiring and investor gatekeeping less relevant, especially for capable young founders who can now be valued directly by users and markets. Big companies are structurally bad at product development and should increasingly acquire early startups, which also serves as a better way to recruit proven teams. The essay urges young people to take greater risks, focus on making things users want, and recognize that the economic “cage” is already open.
key_points: Lower startup costs shift power from employers and investors toward founders, especially young ones.
Undergrads are undervalued because organizations average out talent and can’t accurately assess early potential.
Acquihiring solves both recruiting and product development problems for big companies.
Riskier career moves pay better on average for the young because time is their main investment.
The core skill gap is not technical ability but learning to make something people want.
money_quotes: The market is a lot more discerning than any employer.  And it is completely non-discriminatory.
The central problem in big companies, and the main reason they're so much less productive than small companies, is the difficulty of valuing each person's work.
The goal in a startup is not to write a cool piece of software.  It's to make something people want.
Your early twenties are exactly the time to take insane career risks.
When companies buy startups, they're effectively fusing recruiting and product development.
key_insight: With startup costs low, young founders should take bigger risks and focus on building what users want, because markets now reward them more accurately than employers or investors.
